content.text: Content Moderation in the Context of Claude

Content moderation refers to the systems and processes that evaluate user-generated content — posts, comments, messages, media — against platform policies and remove or restrict content that violates them. Claude can serve two different roles in content moderation: as the
(Claude's own outputs need to comply with policies) and as a
(Claude can classify and evaluate user-generated content on other platforms).
This document focuses on building content moderation systems

Claude. These are among the most valuable applications of LLMs: content that used to require expensive human review can be classified at scale, with semantic understanding that keyword filters and simpler ML models cannot match.

attrs.alt: Content moderation pipeline: pre-publish, post-publish, and continuous monitoring phases
attrs.title: Content Moderation Pipeline

content.text: Content moderation has historically relied on two approaches, both with serious limitations:
Simple character substitution (5ex, h@te) defeats keyword filters. Violators adapt faster than filters can be updated.
Blocklisting common words in sensitive topics blocks legitimate speech. "kill" in gaming context vs. threat context is indistinguishable to a keyword filter.
"I want to kill this level" vs. "I want to kill you" look identical to a keyword match on "kill you" with limited context.
Trained classifiers degrade when language evolves — new slang, new coded language, cross-language code-switching.
Building a good classifier requires large labeled datasets, which are expensive and introduce human biases.
The cases where a classifier is most needed (nuanced, context-dependent) are exactly where it has least confidence.
LLMs used as classifiers address these limitations because they have semantic understanding of language and context at a level that neither keyword filters nor narrow ML models can match.
Most platform content policies cover similar core categories, though thresholds vary by platform type:
Label or remove based on harm

The "context matters" column is critical: nearly every category has legitimate exceptions where the same surface content is acceptable in one context and violating in another. This is where LLM-based moderation has the largest advantage over rules-based approaches.
Using Claude as a Content Moderator
The core pattern: provide Claude with a moderation system prompt, a content policy, and the content to evaluate. Request structured output (JSON) for reliable downstream processing:

# Content moderation with Claude as the classifier
# Shows the core pattern: LLM-based moderation for semantic understanding

import anthropic
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

class ModerationCategory(Enum):
    SAFE = "safe"
    HARASSMENT = "harassment"
    HATE_SPEECH = "hate_speech"
    SEXUAL = "sexual"
    VIOLENCE = "violence"
    SPAM = "spam"
    SELF_HARM = "self_harm"
    MISINFORMATION = "misinformation"

@dataclass
class ModerationResult:
    safe: bool
    categories: list[str]          # violated categories
    severity: str                   # "none" | "low" | "medium" | "high"
    action: str                     # "allow" | "review" | "remove"
    explanation: str
    confidence: float

MODERATION_SYSTEM = """You are a content moderation assistant.
Analyze the provided text for policy violations.

Categories to check:
- harassment: Targeted attacks on individuals
- hate_speech: Content attacking protected characteristics  
- sexual: Explicit sexual content (flag if unsolicited/minors involved)
- violence: Graphic depictions of violence or incitement
- spam: Repetitive, promotional, or coordinated inauthentic content
- self_harm: Content promoting self-harm or suicide
- misinformation: Demonstrably false claims about health, elections, etc.

For each piece of content, respond with JSON:
{
  "safe": true/false,
  "categories": ["list", "of", "violated", "categories"],
  "severity": "none|low|medium|high",
  "action": "allow|review|remove",
  "explanation": "brief reason",
  "confidence": 0.0-1.0
}

Severity guide:
- none: no violations
- low: borderline, context-dependent
- medium: clear violation, policy context matters
- high: severe violation, remove immediately

Action guide:
- allow: no action needed
- review: human should check before decision
- remove: clear policy violation, should be removed
"""

def moderate_content(text: str, platform_context: Optional[str] = None) -> ModerationResult:
    """
    Moderate a piece of content using Claude as the semantic classifier.
    
    platform_context: Optional context about the platform's specific policies
    (e.g., "adult platform with age verification" or "children's platform")
    """
    user_content = f"Content to moderate:\n\n{text}"
    if platform_context:
        user_content = f"Platform context: {platform_context}\n\n{user_content}"
    
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=256,
        system=MODERATION_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_content}],
    )
    
    data = json.loads(response.content[0].text.strip())
    return ModerationResult(
        safe=data.get("safe", True),
        categories=data.get("categories", []),
        severity=data.get("severity", "none"),
        action=data.get("action", "allow"),
        explanation=data.get("explanation", ""),
        confidence=float(data.get("confidence", 0.9)),
    )

# Test with sample content
test_cases = [
    ("Great product! Five stars! ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐", None),
    ("I hate all [group] people, they should not exist", None),
    ("Anyone know a good recipe for banana bread?", None),
    ("Click here to claim your prize! Send $100 first", None),
]

print("Content Moderation Results:")
print("=" * 60)

for text, context in test_cases:
    # Note: In production you would call moderate_content(text, context)
    # Here we show the pattern without making actual API calls
    print(f"\nContent: {text[:60]!r}")
    print(f"  [Would call: moderate_content(text, context={context!r})]")
    print(f"  Expected output: ModerationResult(safe=..., categories=[...], action='allow|review|remove')")

print("\nPattern complete. In production, integrate into content pipeline.")
A well-designed moderation system prompt includes:
Precise definitions of each policy category with examples of what is and is not a violation.
How to distinguish low/medium/high severity within a category.
What severity levels map to which actions (allow/review/remove).
How to account for platform context, author context, and newsworthiness.
JSON with defined fields for downstream processing.
The same content may be appropriate on one platform and violating on another. Pass platform context to the moderator:

# Same content, different platform contexts
content = "Detailed guide to harm reduction for recreational drug use"

# Adult harm reduction platform
result_a = moderate_content(
    content,
    platform_context="Adult harm reduction and drug education platform. "
                     "Users have acknowledged they are 18+."
)
# result_a.action → likely "allow"

# General social media
result_b = moderate_content(
    content,
    platform_context="General social media platform with users of all ages."
)
# result_b.action → likely "review" or "remove"
Content is screened before it becomes visible to other users. Necessary for:
Platforms with strict content policies where any violation is unacceptable
Content that reaches a large audience immediately (trending, featured)
Minors' platforms where any slip-through is higher risk

: every publish action waits for the moderation call to complete. For comment systems, this can be noticeable. Mitigate with fast pre-filters (structural rules, keyword scan) that short-circuit before the LLM call.
Content is published immediately and reviewed asynchronously. The LLM classifier runs in a background queue and can take more time, use a larger model, and consider more context.
Can use a more capable (and slower) model than the synchronous gate
Can revisit the decision with additional context (user history, engagement patterns)
Catches content that evolved through user interaction (comments that become harassment threads)
Systems that learn from patterns over time, detect coordinated behavior, and improve accuracy:
Coordinated inauthentic behavior detection (multiple accounts posting similar content simultaneously)
Account-level pattern analysis (repeated minor violations, ban evasion)
Classifier accuracy measurement — comparing automated decisions to human review outcomes
Policy evolution — feeding real-world cases back into classifier training or prompt updates

Content moderation systems face a fundamental precision-recall tradeoff: being more aggressive reduces harmful content but increases false positives (wrongly removing legitimate speech). The right balance depends on:

A children's education platform has very low tolerance for false negatives (content that slips through). A professional discussion forum prioritizes false positives (limiting legitimate speech) more carefully.
A viral post warrants more aggressive moderation than a comment on a niche forum. Apply stricter thresholds to high-reach content.
Self-harm and CSAM-adjacent content warrant near-zero false negative tolerance. Spam tolerance can be higher — missed spam is annoying, not dangerous.
If your false positive rate is high, your appeals queue will be overwhelmed. Monitor both false positives and false negatives and tune accordingly.

A practical calibration approach: start with a high threshold (many goes to human review), measure human reviewer agreement rates, then lower the automation threshold where the model agrees with humans >95% of the time. This builds in automatic quality gates.

Fully automated moderation is not appropriate for decisions with significant consequences — account bans, legal reporting, or removal of content with newsworthiness value. A well-designed system routes borderline and high-stakes decisions to human review:
Content that the classifier marks as medium severity or low confidence should go to humans, not be auto-removed.
Users should be able to appeal automated removals. Appeals by definition go to humans.
Decisions about journalists, politicians, or organizations with large audiences warrant human oversight.
When the classifier encounters content in a new category or with unusual features, route to humans to build the training data for future improvements.

Content moderation with Claude uses LLM semantic understanding to classify user-generated content against platform policies — addressing the context-blindness of keyword filters and the poor generalization of narrow ML classifiers. A complete system has three phases: pre-publish screening (synchronous, adds latency but blocks violations before they appear), post-publish async classification (no latency cost, more thorough), and continuous monitoring (detects coordinated behavior, measures accuracy, improves policies). Calibrate precision-recall thresholds by category and reach, route borderline decisions to human review, and build feedback loops so that human decisions improve automated accuracy over time.
